column1: The {{c1::Balance Sheet}} is a snapshot of a company's assets, liabilities, and equity at a specific point in time.
column2: It provides the fundamental equation: Assets = Liabilities + Equity.

column1: The {{c1::Income Statement}} details a company's revenues, expenses, and net income over a specific period.
The Statement of Cash Flows tracks three main types of activities: {{c1::Operating}}, {{c2::Investing}}, and {{c3::Financing}}.
column2: Operating (e.g., wages), Investing (e.g., new buildings), and Financing (e.g., bank loans).

column1: The DuPont framework breaks ROE into three components: {{c1::Profitability}} (controlling expenses), {{c2::Efficiency}} (utilizing assets), and {{c3::Leverage}} (borrowing money).
column2: This detailed analysis helps draw conclusions about business operations.

column1: The {{c1::operating cycle}} is the time it takes to buy inventory, sell it, and collect the cash.
column2: A lengthy cycle ties up cash and can cause shortfalls.

column1: {{c1::Variable costs}} change based on production volume, while {{c2::fixed costs}} remain constant regardless of production.
column2: E.g., materials are variable; rent is fixed.

column1: Under {{c1::Responsibility Accounting}}, individuals are held accountable only for the financial inflows and outflows they can control.
column2: This concept is key when creating a budget.
